questions.title: Question 1
questions.question: **What primary key constraint must a feature table have for Databricks Feature Engineering in UC?**

choices.A: No constraint — any column can serve as the key
choices.B: Must declare a primary key column to enable point-in-time lookups and online serving
choices.D: Must be a numeric integer type

questions.shortAnswer: Feature tables in UC require a declared primary key column. This enables entity-based lookups at serving time and supports point-in-time correct joins during training set creation.

questions.title: Question 2
questions.question: **A team wants to compute and register features for reuse across multiple ML projects. Which API should they use?**

choices.B: `databricks.feature_engineering.FeatureEngineeringClient`

questions.shortAnswer: `FeatureEngineeringClient` is the correct API for managing Databricks Feature Engineering in Unity Catalog feature tables.

questions.title: Question 3
questions.question: **`FeatureEngineeringClient.create_training_set()` returns which object?**

choices.B: A `FeatureEngineeringTrainingSet` that must be converted with `.load_df()`

questions.shortAnswer: `create_training_set()` returns a `FeatureEngineeringTrainingSet`. Call `.load_df()` on it to get the actual training DataFrame.

questions.title: Question 4
questions.question: **A model deployed via `fe.log_model()` is served from an endpoint. How does the endpoint retrieve feature values for incoming requests?**

choices.A: The client application must include all feature values in every request
choices.B: The endpoint automatically retrieves features from the online store using the entity key in the request
choices.C: A separate feature computation pipeline must run before each prediction
choices.D: Features are cached at endpoint startup

questions.shortAnswer: Feature Engineering in UC automatically links the model to its feature tables; at serving time, the endpoint retrieves features from the online store using only the entity key.

questions.title: Question 5
questions.question: **What is "point-in-time correct" feature retrieval?**

choices.A: Retrieving the most recently computed feature values
choices.B: Retrieving feature values that were available at the time of each label's event timestamp, preventing future data leakage
choices.C: Retrieving features refreshed within the past hour
choices.D: Retrieving features at exactly midnight each day

questions.shortAnswer: Point-in-time correct retrieval joins features to labels using timestamps so that only data available before the label's event time is used, preventing data leakage.

questions.title: Question 6
questions.question: **Which `FeatureLookup` parameter enables point-in-time correct retrieval?**

choices.B: `feature_names`

questions.shortAnswer: `timestamp_lookup_key` specifies the column to use as the event timestamp for point-in-time joins.

questions.title: Question 7
questions.question: **A feature requires < 10ms retrieval latency for real-time serving. Where should the feature values be published?**

choices.B: Online store (Redis-backed)

questions.shortAnswer: The online store provides millisecond-latency access required for real-time inference. The Delta offline store is suitable for batch and training, not real-time serving.

questions.title: Question 8
questions.question: **Train-serving skew is caused by:**

choices.A: The model being retrained too frequently
choices.B: Feature computation logic differing between the training pipeline and the serving pipeline
choices.C: The online store going offline during serving
choices.D: Using a stale version of the model

questions.shortAnswer: Train-serving skew occurs when features are computed differently at training time vs serving time, causing the model to see different data distributions than it was trained on.

questions.title: Question 9
questions.question: **A data scientist calls `fe.write_table(df=features_df, name="catalog.schema.user_features")` and the table does not yet exist. What happens?**

choices.A: An error is raised — must call `create_table()` first
choices.B: The feature table is created automatically with schema inferred from the DataFrame
choices.C: The DataFrame is written as a regular unmanaged Delta table
choices.D: The call fails unless the online store is configured

questions.shortAnswer: `write_table` creates the feature table if it doesn't exist, inferring the schema from the DataFrame.

questions.title: Question 10
questions.question: **A data scientist runs Hyperopt with `Trials()` on a 20-node Databricks cluster. How many trials run in parallel?**

choices.B: 1 — `Trials` runs locally on the driver node only
choices.C: Based on number of CPU cores on the driver

questions.shortAnswer: `Trials` is a local, single-process object. Use `SparkTrials` to distribute Hyperopt across the cluster.

questions.title: Question 11
questions.question: **What must the Hyperopt objective function return to signal a failed trial?**

choices.B: `{'loss': float('inf'), 'status': STATUS_OK}`

questions.shortAnswer: Return `{'status': STATUS_FAIL}` to signal a failed trial without crashing the entire search. The trial is skipped and the next configuration is tried.

questions.title: Question 12
questions.question: **Which `hp.*` function is best for tuning a learning rate over the range 1e-5 to 1e-1?**

choices.B: `hp.loguniform('lr', np.log(1e-5), np.log(1e-1))`

questions.shortAnswer: Log-uniform search allocates equal probability to each order of magnitude. `hp.uniform` would concentrate most trials near the upper end of the range.

questions.title: Question 13
questions.question: **`mlflow.autolog()` is enabled and Hyperopt runs 50 trials with `SparkTrials`. How are MLflow runs organized?**

choices.B: 1 parent run with 50 child runs (one per trial)
choices.D: No runs created — autolog doesn't support Hyperopt

questions.shortAnswer: MLflow autolog with Hyperopt creates a parent run for the `fmin()` call and a child run for each individual trial.

questions.title: Question 14
questions.question: **A `CrossValidator` with `numFolds=5` evaluates a parameter grid with 12 combinations. How many models are trained total?**
questions.shortAnswer: Each of the 12 parameter combinations is trained on 5 folds = 60 models total.

questions.title: Question 15
questions.question: **When does Bayesian optimization (TPE) outperform random search most significantly?**

choices.A: When the search space is very small (< 10 combinations)
choices.B: When compute budget is limited relative to the search space size
choices.D: When the objective function is convex

questions.shortAnswer: Bayesian optimization shines when you have limited trials: it uses results from past trials to prioritize promising regions, whereas random search wastes budget on unpromising areas.

questions.title: Question 16
questions.question: **What does `parallelism=8` in `SparkTrials(parallelism=8)` control?**

choices.A: Number of Spark partitions per trial
choices.B: Number of trials that run simultaneously across the cluster
choices.C: Number of MLflow child runs per trial

questions.shortAnswer: `parallelism` in SparkTrials controls how many Hyperopt trials execute concurrently across the Spark cluster.

questions.title: Question 17
questions.question: **The `early_stop_fn` parameter in `fmin()` allows:**

choices.A: Stopping individual trials that exceed a time limit
choices.B: Stopping the entire search early if improvement has stalled

questions.shortAnswer: `early_stop_fn` is called after each trial and can signal `fmin()` to stop searching early based on custom logic (e.g., no improvement in last N trials).

questions.title: Question 18
questions.question: **A team has budget for 30 trials to tune 6 hyperparameters. Which strategy is most efficient?**

choices.B: Random search

questions.shortAnswer: With 30 trials over 6 parameters, Bayesian optimization uses previous results to guide the search, typically finding better configurations than random search in the same budget.

questions.title: Question 19
questions.question: **Which URI loads a model from the UC registry by alias?**

choices.B: `models:/ml_catalog.fraud_models.fraud_classifier@champion`

questions.shortAnswer: UC model URIs use the full three-level namespace followed by `@alias`.

questions.title: Question 20
questions.question: **A serving endpoint with `scale_to_zero_enabled=True` hasn't received traffic for 45 minutes. What happens to the next request?**

choices.A: The request fails with a 503 error
choices.B: The request succeeds after a 60-120 second cold start delay
choices.C: The request returns from cache immediately

questions.shortAnswer: Scale-to-zero deprovisions compute when idle. The first request after idle must wait for cluster startup, typically 60-120 seconds.

questions.title: Question 21
questions.question: **`mlflow.pyfunc.spark_udf()` runs inference on which Spark component?**

choices.B: Spark executors (distributed across the cluster)

questions.shortAnswer: `spark_udf` distributes inference across Spark executors, enabling parallel scoring of millions of rows.

questions.title: Question 22
questions.question: **A model is registered to the workspace registry (no `set_registry_uri` call). Which UC feature is unavailable?**

choices.B: Version tags
choices.C: UC lineage tracking and cross-catalog permissions

questions.shortAnswer: UC lineage and GRANT/REVOKE on model objects require the UC registry. The workspace registry supports aliases and tags but not UC governance features.

questions.title: Question 23
questions.question: **Which statement about model aliases is TRUE?**

choices.A: Each model can have only one alias at a time
choices.B: Aliases are named pointers — changing the pointed-to version doesn't require updating application code
choices.D: Aliases are equivalent to legacy lifecycle stages

questions.shortAnswer: This is the core value of aliases: application code uses `models:/name@champion`, and promotion is just reassigning the alias to a different version.

questions.title: Question 24
questions.question: **An endpoint has champion at 80% and challenger at 10% traffic. Is this a valid configuration?**

choices.A: Yes — challenger traffic below 50% is fine
choices.B: No — percentages must sum to exactly 100%
choices.C: Yes — Databricks distributes the remaining 10% randomly
choices.D: No — only two served models are allowed per endpoint

questions.shortAnswer: Traffic config percentages must sum to exactly 100%. The configuration described (80% + 10% = 90%) is invalid.

questions.title: Question 25
questions.question: **Which deployment pattern runs the challenger model in parallel without serving its predictions to users?**

choices.B: Blue-green deployment

questions.shortAnswer: Shadow deployment sends all traffic to the champion but also runs the challenger silently, logging its predictions for offline comparison with zero user impact.

questions.title: Question 26
questions.question: **What enables automatic logging of all requests and responses from a serving endpoint to a Delta table?**

choices.B: `AutoCaptureConfigInput` in the endpoint configuration
choices.D: Delta Streaming from the endpoint logs

questions.shortAnswer: `AutoCaptureConfigInput` (inference tables) is configured on the serving endpoint and automatically logs all request/response payloads to a Delta table.

questions.title: Question 27
questions.question: **A `PythonModel` subclass needs to load a pre-trained tokenizer at serving time. The tokenizer is stored locally as a file. How should it be packaged with the model?**

choices.A: Hardcode the file path in the `predict()` method
choices.B: Pass it as an environment variable
choices.C: Include it in the `artifacts` dict in `mlflow.pyfunc.log_model()` and load it in `load_context()`
choices.D: Store it in DBFS and access it from the model

questions.shortAnswer: The `artifacts` dict in `log_model()` specifies local files to bundle with the model; `load_context()` receives the resolved paths via `context.artifacts`.

questions.title: Question 28
questions.question: **Which task dependency setting ensures an integration test task runs only if the evaluation gate task succeeds?**

choices.B: `run_if: ALL_SUCCESS` with `depends_on: [evaluation_gate]`
choices.D: No special configuration — tasks always wait for predecessors

questions.shortAnswer: `run_if: ALL_SUCCESS` plus the `depends_on` dependency ensures the task runs only when all upstream tasks have completed successfully.

questions.title: Question 29
questions.question: **Drift-triggered retraining uses which mechanism to start a Databricks Job?**

choices.B: SQL alert → webhook → Databricks Jobs REST API
choices.C: Auto-trigger based on MLflow metric threshold
choices.D: Git push to the model training branch

questions.shortAnswer: Monitoring alerts (Databricks SQL alerts or Lakehouse Monitoring alerts) fire webhooks to external systems; the webhook calls the Databricks Jobs REST API to trigger a retraining run.

questions.title: Question 30
questions.question: **For real-time fraud detection requiring < 50ms response time, which deployment mode is correct?**

choices.B: REST endpoint with `scale_to_zero_enabled=False`

questions.shortAnswer: REST endpoint provides sub-100ms latency; disabling scale_to_zero eliminates cold start risk for latency-sensitive workloads.

questions.title: Question 31
questions.question: **A custom pyfunc model returns predictions as a DataFrame with columns `[score, label]`. What should `result_type` be in `spark_udf()`?**

choices.B: `"string"`

questions.shortAnswer: When the pyfunc returns a multi-column DataFrame, `result_type` must be a Spark `StructType` matching the output columns.

questions.title: Question 32
questions.question: **What is the purpose of archiving the previous `champion` alias as `previous_champion` during promotion?**

choices.B: Enables quick rollback by setting `champion` back to the `previous_champion` version
choices.C: Prevents the old version from being deleted

questions.shortAnswer: Keeping a `previous_champion` alias allows one-command rollback: `client.set_registered_model_alias(name, "champion", prev_version)`.

questions.title: Question 33
questions.question: **Feature PSI = 0.05 this week. What action should you take?**

choices.B: Investigate the upstream data pipeline
choices.C: No action — PSI < 0.10 indicates stable population

questions.shortAnswer: PSI < 0.10 means no significant population shift. Continue regular monitoring cadence.

questions.title: Question 34
questions.question: **A model's AUC dropped from 0.92 to 0.84 over 3 weeks with ground truth labels available. Which drift type explains this?**

choices.B: Prediction drift — outputs changed
choices.C: Concept drift — the input-output relationship changed
choices.D: Label drift — target class balance shifted

questions.shortAnswer: Measurable performance degradation (AUC drop) with ground truth labels indicates concept drift: the relationship between features and the target has changed.

questions.title: Question 35
questions.question: **Which column in a Databricks inference table identifies which model version handled each request (for A/B analysis)?**

choices.B: `served_model_name`

questions.shortAnswer: The `served_model_name` field (extracted from the request JSON) identifies which served model configuration handled each request.

questions.title: Question 36
questions.question: **Which Lakehouse Monitoring monitor type is appropriate for a static, snapshot Delta table with no timestamp column?**

choices.B: InferenceLog

questions.shortAnswer: Snapshot monitors compute statistics on the entire table as-is, without time-window partitioning.

questions.title: Question 37
questions.question: **UC audit logs capture which ML-related event?**

choices.B: Hyperopt trial results

questions.shortAnswer: UC audit logs capture governance events including alias updates (promotions), permission grants, and model registrations.

questions.title: Question 38
questions.question: **The GDPR Article 22 requirement that affects ML models is:**

choices.A: Data must be encrypted at rest
choices.B: Models must be retrained every 6 months
choices.C: Individuals have the right to meaningful explanation of automated decisions affecting them
choices.D: Predictions must be stored for 7 years

questions.shortAnswer: Article 22 grants individuals the right to not be subject to solely automated decisions and requires meaningful explanation. SHAP values are the standard technical control.

questions.title: Question 39
questions.question: **Equalized odds as a fairness metric requires:**

choices.B: Equal true positive rates and equal false positive rates across groups
choices.D: Equal training data representation across groups

questions.shortAnswer: Equalized odds (Hardt et al.) requires both TPR and FPR to be equal across protected groups, making it stricter than demographic parity (which only requires equal positive prediction rates).

questions.title: Question 40
questions.question: **A model has PSI = 0.28 on prediction scores (not features). What does this indicate?**

choices.B: Input features have drifted
choices.C: Model output distribution has shifted significantly — potential concept drift or upstream data change
choices.D: The online store is returning stale values

questions.shortAnswer: High PSI on prediction scores indicates the model is producing very different outputs than it did against the baseline. Investigate whether upstream data or the concept has changed.

questions.title: Question 41
questions.question: **Which UC permission allows a data scientist to register new model versions but NOT change the `champion` alias?**

choices.B: `SELECT ON MODEL`
choices.C: `MODIFY ON MODEL` (both registering and alias setting require MODIFY — use process controls)

questions.shortAnswer: Both registering new versions and setting aliases require `MODIFY`. There is no separate UC permission to separate these; process-level controls (PR review, approval workflows) enforce the distinction.

questions.title: Question 42
questions.question: **A Delta table contains PII (email addresses) that must be hidden from data scientists but visible to the ML platform team. Which control should be used?**

choices.B: Delta column masking policy
choices.D: Encrypt the column with a user-specific key

questions.shortAnswer: Delta column masking applies a masking function at query time, showing masked values to unauthorized users while platform team queries see real values.

questions.title: Question 43
questions.question: **Databricks Lakehouse Monitoring outputs a `_drift_metrics` table. What columns does this table contain?**

choices.B: Statistical drift measures (e.g., KS statistic, chi-square, PSI) per feature per time window compared to baseline
choices.D: MLflow run IDs for the monitored model

questions.shortAnswer: The drift metrics table contains per-feature, per-window drift statistics comparing the current window profile to the baseline profile.

questions.title: Question 44
questions.question: **After retraining a model and promoting it to champion, what must be updated in Lakehouse Monitoring?**

choices.B: The baseline table — update it to reflect the new model's training distribution
choices.D: Nothing — LHM automatically detects the new champion

questions.shortAnswer: The baseline table should be updated after a significant model change (new training data, new features) so that drift metrics compare against the new model's expected distribution, not the old one.

questions.title: Question 45
questions.question: **A compliance evidence package for a high-risk ML model must include which item?**

choices.A: Source code for all Spark jobs
choices.B: Bias test results demonstrating the model meets fairness thresholds
choices.C: A list of all MLflow experiments in the workspace

questions.shortAnswer: Bias and fairness test results are critical compliance evidence for high-risk models, demonstrating that the model was evaluated for discriminatory impact before deployment.
